title: Toward a Theory of Prime Detecting Sieves
speaker: Kevin Ford
abstract: Given a set of integers, we wish to know how many primes there are in the set.  Modern tools allow us to obtain an asymptotic for the number of primes, or at least a lower bound of the expected order, assuming certain strength Type-I information (the distribution of the sequence in progressions) and Type-II information (bilinear sums over the sequence). The methods used previously, especially Harman's sieve, are largely ad-hoc and shed little light on the limitations of the methods.

proof_stages.label: Classical sieve setup and parity obstruction
proof_stages.summary: The talk formulates the problem of counting primes in a finite set, reviews the exact Mobius-inclusion formula and its approximate sieve replacement, and explains that Type-I distribution up to even nearly x is not by itself prime-detecting.

proof_stages.label: Weighted Type I/Type II formulation
proof_stages.summary: The set is replaced by a normalized nonnegative weight, Type I distribution and uniform bilinear Type II bounds are introduced with parameters gamma, theta, and nu, and the extremal prime-detection constant c(theta,nu,gamma) is defined.

proof_stages.label: Need for a systematic extremal theory
proof_stages.summary: Known successes and standard prime decomposition identities are presented as effective but ad hoc, motivating a direct method that deploys all Type I and Type II information simultaneously and should make the constants finitely computable.

proof_stages.label: Complete asymptotic criterion and parameter reductions
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem gives necessary and sufficient parameter conditions for the expected prime asymptotic, after reductions to theta at most one half and to gamma lying outside the half-open Type II range.

proof_stages.label: Zero-constant obstruction below level one half
proof_stages.summary: For genuinely gamma<1/2, an exponent alpha outside the Type II interval is chosen and a nonnegative weight supported away from primes but compatible with the available Type I and Type II data is constructed, proving that the prime-detection constant can be zero.

proof_stages.label: Linnik identity reduction to a residual set
proof_stages.summary: A Linnik prime-decomposition identity is substituted for the prime indicator, Type I information truncates its divisors, and Type II information removes integers with divisors in the bilinear range, leaving a residual set U whose negligibility gives the asymptotic.

proof_stages.label: Vector encoding and dual optimization
proof_stages.summary: Integers are encoded by vectors of logarithmic prime-factor sizes, subset-sum conditions describe the Type II exclusions, and nonnegative test functions H_SL satisfying a dual condition give optimizable lower bounds that are sharp when matching vanishing weights can be built.

proof_stages.label: Theta-family sharpness computation
proof_stages.summary: In a simplified parameter range arising from fractional parts of alpha p, the residual set reduces to four-prime configurations, the optimal H-function is found via a pair-complement symmetry, and sharpness is tied to solving nonlinear Volterra integral equations for the corresponding weight construction.

examples.name: Parity-obstruction set with an even number of prime factors
examples.note: This set has essentially optimal distribution in progressions but contains no primes, visibly forcing the move from Type-I-only sieving to extra bilinear information.
examples.engagement_reason: He cites the even-number-of-prime-factors set as the parity-obstruction counterexample and states its distribution/no-primes facts without working through it.

passes.reason: It has essentially best-possible distribution while containing no primes, and is used to refute the sufficiency of type-one distribution alone.

examples.name: Friedlander-Iwaniec primes of the form square plus fourth power
examples.note: The result illustrates that adding Type II information to a gamma=3/4 Type I level can yield an asymptotic formula for primes in a sparse sequence.
examples.engagement_reason: He lists Friedlander-Iwaniec square-plus-fourth-power primes as a known success with certain sieve parameters, but does no calculation on that example.

passes.reason: The square-plus-fourth-power primes are a thin but systematic polynomial-form family used as a successful asymptotic application.

examples.name: Maynard's primes with missing digits
examples.note: This sparse digit-restricted prime problem illustrates a case where weaker Type I and Type II data still give a positive lower bound of the expected order.
examples.engagement_reason: He cites Maynard’s missing-digits primes and gives the parameter values/lower-bound outcome, without applying or deriving anything for the example.

examples.name: One-parameter family with gamma=1/2 and theta=0
examples.note: Varying nu around the Duke-Friedlander endpoint illustrates how the criterion and optimization produce exact constants, positivity ranges, and zero-constant thresholds.
examples.engagement_reason: He applies the criterion to the gamma=1/2, theta=0 case, checks the relevant conditions for nu=1/3, varies nu through the family, and later works through a concrete construction with Linick factors and support conditions.

passes.reason: The parameter family lies on the critical gamma=1/2 boundary and is used to illustrate threshold and exact-constant phenomena.
